metadata.title: One More Touchpoint (+5): Newsletter, Social Set, or Pitch Template
metadata.playlist: Ship It — Build Your Brand's Live Presence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 8 brief — Building Your Live Website, Resumes & LinkedIn Header

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, in for Bear. Episode eight, the bonus. Five free points for proving your brand system stretches to a fourth surface. You have built a website, two resumes, and a LinkedIn header. Now you pick one more: an email or newsletter template, a set of three or more social posts, or a pitch deck template. Same brand tokens as everything else — same background color, same accent, same font, same voice. One more proof that the system is a system and not a collection of one-off design decisions.

props.segment: One More Touchpoint (+5): Newsletter, Social Set, or Pitch Template
props.command: Extend the brand system to a fourth surface — one template, same tokens, +5 pts.
output: Option A: Email / newsletter template — repeatable layout in brand colors and font.
Option B: 3+ social posts — same style tag, same palette, same format.
Option C: Pitch deck template — 5-slide framework in your brand system.
Same brand tokens · one new surface · +5 pts

beats.act: Act 1 — pick one surface and commit
beats.narration_text: Three options. Pick one. Option one: an email or newsletter template. A repeatable layout for a Substack, a newsletter, or a cold-outreach email — brand colors, brand font, a consistent header and footer. Most useful if you are building a content brand where regular email is the primary channel. Option two: three or more social posts in a consistent style — same color palette, same font treatment, same format dimension, whether that is square at 1080 by 1080 pixels or portrait at 1080 by 1350. Not three different experiments — three posts that clearly belong to the same account. Most useful if you are building social presence as part of your brand. Option three: a five-slide pitch deck template — cover, problem, solution, proof, and call to action — built in your brand system. Most useful if you are launching a startup, a product, or a service and you will be presenting to others. Pick the one that matches what you are actually building. Do not attempt all three. A half-finished social set and a rough email template submitted together is worth less than one clean, complete deliverable.

artifactLines: Option A — Email / newsletter template: repeatable header, body, footer layout in brand colors and font. Best for: content brands where email is the primary channel.
Option B — 3+ social posts: same palette, same font treatment, same format (1080×1080 square or 1080×1350 portrait). Best for: brands where social presence is the primary channel.
Option C — 5-slide pitch deck template: Cover → Problem → Solution → Proof → CTA, built in your brand system. Best for: startups, products, and service launches.
Pick one. A half-finished social set + a rough email template together is worth less than one complete deliverable.

beats.act: Act 2 — apply the same brand tokens
beats.narration_text: Here is the generation prompt — shown for the email template, but the structure works for any of the three options. Swap the surface type in the brackets and adjust the format line to match. 'Create a [surface type: email template, social post set, or five-slide pitch deck template]. Brand system: background color [your primary hex], accent color [your accent hex], font [your font name], brand voice adjective [your adjective — for example, direct and warm, or rigorous and clear]. Purpose: [one sentence describing what this surface is for — for example, a monthly update for design professionals, or a pitch for an AI productivity tool for students]. Produce a clean, reusable template — headings, body text, and one call to action — in Markdown for an email template, or describe the layout clearly for slides, or provide a visual prompt I can use for social image generation.' The key word in that prompt is reusable. The deliverable is a template you can fill in repeatedly, not a one-time finished post.

props.command: Create a [SURFACE TYPE: email template / social post set / 5-slide pitch deck template]. Brand: background [PRIMARY_HEX], accent [ACCENT_HEX], font [FONT_NAME], voice [BRAND_ADJECTIVE — e.g., 'direct and warm', 'rigorous and clear']. Purpose: [ONE SENTENCE describing what this surface is for]. Produce a clean, reusable template — headings, body text, and one call-to-action — in Markdown (for email) / describe the layout clearly (for slides) / provide a visual prompt I can use for image generation (for social).
output: Template draft: header, body sections, footer — all placeholders clearly labeled.
Brand tokens applied: background hex, accent hex, font name, voice adjective.
Format: Markdown for email / layout description for slides / prompt for social.

beats.narration_text: Before you submit, run the consistency test. Open three things side by side: your website, one of your resumes, and the new bonus touchpoint. Answer three questions. First: same color palette? Check that the background and accent colors are identical hex values — or at minimum that the tones are clearly from the same family. Second: same or complementary fonts? Your brand font should appear in the same role — headings in the heading font, body in the body font — across all three surfaces. Third: does reading them feel like the same brand? This is the voice question. If the website copy is confident and direct, and the email template is apologetic and hedging, the brand is inconsistent even if the colors match. If any answer is no, find the specific element that drifted and fix it before submitting. The bonus points are for demonstrating that the system is consistent, not just that you produced a fourth deliverable.

artifactLines: Open side by side: your website + one resume + the new bonus touchpoint.
Check 1 — Same color palette? Background and accent hex values must match (or be clearly the same tonal family) across all three.
Check 2 — Same or complementary fonts? Brand font in the same role: headings as headings, body as body, on every surface.
Check 3 — Same brand voice? Read a sentence from each. If the tone shifts from confident to hedging between surfaces, the voice drifted.
If any answer is No: identify the specific drifted element and fix it before submitting. Consistency is the grade, not completion.

beats.narration_text: The bonus rules. Pick one surface and execute it completely — one well-built email template beats three half-finished experiments every time. Apply the exact same brand tokens from Assignments 6 and 7: same background hex, same accent hex, same font, same voice adjective in the copy. Run the consistency test before submitting — website, resume, and touchpoint open side by side, three checks, fix whatever drifted. Submit one PDF containing the deliverable. The five points are for proving the brand system scales, not for volume.

artifactLines: Pick ONE surface and do it well: email template, 3+ social posts, or 5-slide pitch deck template.
Apply the exact same brand tokens: background hex, accent hex, font, voice adjective — no drift from A6/A7.
Consistency test before submitting: website + resume + touchpoint side by side, three checks — color, font, voice.
Submit one PDF. The 5 points are for proving the system scales, not for producing volume.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the complete prompt — fill in every bracket with your actual brand values from your A6/A7 brand sheet before you send it. 'I am creating [surface type: email template, social post set, or five-slide pitch deck template] for Assignment 8, bonus touchpoint. My brand system from Assignments 6 and 7: background color [your hex], accent color [your hex], font [your font name], brand voice adjective [your adjective]. Purpose of this surface: [one sentence — what it is for and who reads it]. Generate a reusable template with placeholder text I can fill in with my actual content. Keep every design decision consistent with my website and resume brand surfaces — same tokens, same hierarchy, same voice.' Read that handoff prompt aloud. You are giving Claude a complete brief, not a vague request. The more precisely you fill in the brackets, the less cleanup you will need on the output.

props.segment: Generate your bonus touchpoint — complete brief
props.command: I'm creating [SURFACE TYPE] for Assignment 8, bonus touchpoint. My brand system from A6/A7: background [HEX], accent [HEX], font [FONT], voice adjective [ADJECTIVE]. Purpose: [ONE SENTENCE — what this surface is for and who reads it]. Generate a reusable template with placeholder text I can fill in with my actual content. Keep every design decision consistent with my website and resume brand surfaces — same tokens, same hierarchy, same voice.
output: Template with labeled placeholder sections — fill in your real content.
All design decisions traced to your stated brand tokens.
Format matched to your chosen surface type.

beats.narration_text: One More Touchpoint. Liam, in for Bear.

props.title: One More Touchpoint (+5): Newsletter, Social Set, or Pitch Template
